201	          ],
   202	          "transitions": [{"type": "cut"}, {"type": "cut"}]
   203	        },
   204	        {
   205	          "kind": "audio",
   206	          "clips": [
   207	            {"source": {"file": "<bgm_audio_path>", "in_ms": 0, "out_ms": 10000}, "start_ms": 0, "volume": 0.35, "fade_in_ms": 2000},
   208	            {"source": {"file": "<bgm_audio_path>", "in_ms": 10000, "out_ms": 20000}, "start_ms": 10000, "volume": 0.10},
   209	            {"source": {"file": "<bgm_audio_path>", "in_ms": 20000, "out_ms": 30000}, "start_ms": 20000, "volume": 0.30, "fade_out_ms": 3000}
   210	          ]
   211	        },
   212	        {
   213	          "kind": "audio",
   214	          "clips": [
   215	            {"source": {"file": "<vo_audio_path>", "in_ms": 0, "out_ms": 6000}, "start_ms": 10000, "volume": 1.0, "dbfs": -16}
   216	          ]
   217	        }
   218	      ]
   219	    }
   220	    ```
   221	
   222	    **Mixing rationale in this example**:
   223	    - seq1: no VO → video volume 1.0, BGM 0.35 (carries arc)
   224	    - seq2: VO overlaps → video volume ducked to 0.4 (preserve SFX as bed), BGM ducked to 0.10 (VO must dominate)
   225	    - seq3: no VO → video volume 1.0, BGM recovers to 0.30
   226	    - VO: normalized with `dbfs: -16` for consistent loudness
   227	    - BGM split into 3 sections with different volumes — not flat throughout
   228	    - All time fields use `_ms` suffix — no frame values
   229	
   230	# Edit Spec Quick Reference
   231	
   232	The `execute_edit_video` tool accepts a JSON object describing the edit structure. **All time units are milliseconds** (`source.in_ms`, `source.out_ms`, `start_ms`, `end_ms`).
   233	
   234	For the complete DSL format, parameter details, keyframe animation patterns, and common errors, see `references/video-editor-tool-guide.md`.
   235	
   236	### Track Types
   237	
   238	| kind | Description |
   239	|------|-------------|
   240	| `"video"` | Sequential video clips with transitions |
   241	| `"audio"` | Audio clips |
   242	
   243	### Clip Fields (all time units are milliseconds)
   244	
   245	| Field | Required | Default | Notes |
   246	|-------|----------|---------|-------|
   247	| source.file | yes for file-backed clips | - | Actual file path returned by the upstream tool |
   248	| source.in_ms | no | 0 | Source start (ms) |
   249	| source.out_ms | no | null | Source end (ms, null = full file) |
   250	| start_ms | no | null | Timeline start (ms, null = sequential) |
   251	| end_ms | no | null | Timeline end (for text/subtitle/overlay/color bounds) |
   252	| source.fps | no | auto | Source fps override |
   253	| speed | no | 1.0 | Speed factor |
   254	| volume | no | 1.0 | Volume multiplier |
   255	| fade_in_ms | no | 0 | Fade-in duration (ms) |
   256	| fade_out_ms | no | 0 | Fade-out duration (ms) |
   257	| crop | no | null | Crop {x, y, w, h} |
   258	| scale | no | null | Scale factor |
   259	
   260	**Keyframe format**: `{"ms": milliseconds, "v": value, "e": "linear"|"ease-in"|"ease-out"|"ease-in-out"}`
   261	
   262	### Transitions (video track only)
   263	
   264	| Format | Description |
   265	|--------|-------------|
   266	| `{"type": "cut"}` | Hard cut |
   267	| `{"type": "crossfade", "duration_ms": N}` | Symmetric crossfade (N ms) |
   268	| `{"type": "crossfade", "in_ms": N, "out_ms": M}` | Asymmetric crossfade |
   269	
   270	Transitions array length must equal `clips.length - 1`.
   271	
   272	### Multi-track
   273	
   274	- **Audio**: Multiple `kind: "audio"` tracks play simultaneously (mix). `start_ms` controls timeline position.
   275	- **Video**: Multiple `kind: "video"` tracks are composited in track order.
   276	- **Audio-only mode**: When only `kind: "audio"` tracks exist (no video), output is `.aac`.
   277	
   278	# Tools
   279	
   280	Tools are provided at runtime. This skill uses:
   281	- `execute_edit_video` — timeline assembly, transitions, audio mixing, non-generative edits (trim, speed, crop, scale, fade, keyframe animation)
   282	- `audio_produce` — TTS/voiceover generation (returns audio file)
   283	- `text_to_music` — BGM generation (prompt mode or composition plan mode)
   284	- `image_generate` — cover image generation (fallback only, when no suitable frame can be extracted)
   285	- `get_file_info` — refresh file info / duration verification
   286	- `ffprobe` — media file probe (duration, resolution, fps)
   287	
   288	When using `execute_edit_video`, follow `references/video-editor-tool-guide.md` for DSL format, parameter patterns, and keyframe animation recipes. For assembly rules (volume hard gate, subtitle render safety, pre-assembly checklist, BGM strategy, mix patterns), see `references/assembly-rules.md`.

1	# Content
     2	
     3	## Contents
     4	
     5	- **0. Scope Boundary** — what this reference covers vs what it doesn't
     6	- **1. VO Content Creation Principles** — writing and rhythm rules
     7	- **2. VO-to-Picture Relationship** — sync modes and timing
     8	- **3. VO-First Production Order** — VO drives the timeline, not the other way around
     9	- **4. Cross-Segment VO Handling** — multi-sequence voice consistency
    10	- **5. Creative Planning Application Guidance** — how VO design feeds into creative scripts
    11	- **6. Voice Catalog And Selection Rules** — voice ID selection and matching
    12	
    13	
    14	## 0. Scope Boundary
    15	
    16	This guide is for **off-screen voiceover / narration only**.
    17	
    18	- If the speaker is an on-screen character talking within the scene, use `dialogue-monologue-design-kb.md`
    19	- If the layer is music, ambience, or action-tied sound, use `audio-design-guide.md`
    20	
    21	Voiceover is **normally** a post-production audio layer. The exception is a **true one-shot final delivery**: one single segment generated once and delivered as-is, with no later multi-segment assembly, extension, or deterministic voice-reuse requirement. Outside that narrow case, do not treat voiceover as co-generated in-sequence speech.
    22	
    23	## 1. VO Content Creation Principles
    24	
    25	### Content Structure
    26	- **Opening (Hook)**: Question / conflict / counter-intuition — grab attention instantly
    27	- **Development (Build)**: Tell the story, present scenarios, build emotional connection
    28	- **Closing (Closure)**: Summary / elevation / call to action / brand philosophy — deliver a sense of completion
    29	
    30	### Language Style
    31	| Style | Traits | Suitable For |
    32	|-------|--------|-------------|
    33	| **Concise & Direct** | Short sentences, no decoration, straight to the point | Product features, call to action, fast pace |
    34	| **Poetic & Lyrical** | Rich rhetoric, deep imagery | Brand stories, emotional content, atmosphere |
    35	| **Conversational** | "You," "we" — like talking to the audience | Approachable brands, lifestyle, social |
    36	| **Professional Narrative** | Objective, rational, information-dense | Tutorials, specialized fields, tech products |
    37	
    38	### Creation Guidelines
    39	- **Emotion markers**: Annotate mood at key sentence endings `[firm]` `[tender]` `[excited]`
    40	- **Semantic completeness**: Each segment expresses a complete thought — avoid unnatural breaks
    41	- **Length adaptation**: Consider visual duration; typically 2–4 short sentences per segment
    42	- **Closing completeness**: The final VO must convey a sense of closure
    43	- **Render-safe wording by default**: Write VO/subtitle copy using stable everyday characters and punctuation first. For Chinese-language subtitles, prefer standard everyday punctuation marks and plain Arabic numerals. Avoid decorative Unicode, emoji, uncommon symbols, mixed-width punctuation tricks, and special pause markers when a normal comma, period, or colon already expresses the same meaning.
    44	- **Replace risky punctuation at the writing stage**: Do not generate subtitle-facing copy that depends on glyph-sensitive punctuation such as repeated em dashes, ellipsis variants used for styling, fancy quotes, arrows, or ornamental separators. Rewrite for the same spoken effect with safer forms instead (for example: `The key is -- small, repeated steps` -> `The key is: small, repeated steps`).
    45	- **Design for speech and render together**: If a punctuation choice is mainly visual styling rather than spoken meaning, remove it upstream in the script instead of relying on downstream subtitle normalization. The clean script should already be safe to feed both TTS and final subtitle render.
    46	
    47	## 2. VO-to-Picture Relationship
    48	
    49	### Synchronization
    50	VO describes what's happening on screen. Direct, clear, easy to understand. Suited for product demos, tutorials.
    51	
    52	### Complementary
    53	VO provides information the visuals can't convey (story / philosophy / data); visuals show the scene. Suited for brand stories, product context.
    54	
    55	### Independent Layer
    56	VO and visuals form two parallel narrative lines — contrast or connection creates deeper meaning. Suited for artistic, metaphorical expression.
    57	
    58	### Emotional Guide
    59	VO is minimal, primarily guiding the emotional tone; visuals carry the main information. Suited for mood/atmosphere videos, music-driven content.
    60	
    61	## 3. VO-First Production Order
    62	
    63	For videos with VO or narration, the VO audio is the timeline anchor. **VO duration should not be compressed; visual timing can be adjusted.** The timeline should follow the less flexible layer.
    64	
    65	1. **Generate VO first** with `audio_produce`, then use `ffprobe` / `get_file_info` to read the actual duration.
    66	2. **Use VO duration as the timing base** to estimate each narration line window.
    67	3. **Plan the timeline around VO duration**: video `out_ms`, `start_ms`, transitions, and BGM duration should follow VO rhythm, not force VO into an undersized visual timeline.
    68	
    69	## 4. Cross-Segment VO Handling
    70	
    71	- **First segment**: Write the full VO content
    72	- **Subsequent segments**: Mark with `"(continued)"` to indicate continuation
    73	- Split at natural sentence boundaries or emotion/theme transition points
    74	
    75	## 5. Creative Planning Application Guidance
    76	
    77	**Describe VO style qualitatively**, e.g.:
    78	- "Young female voice, warm and friendly tone, conversational feel like talking to a friend"
    79	- "Mature male voice, professional and authoritative tone, conveying trustworthiness"
    80	
    81	**Describe VO's role**, e.g.:
    82	- "VO drives narrative progression, pushing the story forward"
    83	- "VO serves as an emotional layer, amplifying the mood of the visuals"
    84	- "VO and visuals complement each other — visuals show the scene, VO tells the philosophy"
    85	
    86	**Output VO content**: Segmented copy aligned with the narrative outline, annotated with timing, tone shifts, and emotional beats
    87	
    88	**Avoid**: Specifying timestamps, exact speech-rate parameters, technical voice-direction notes
    89	**Focus**: VO's overall style and emotional tone, relationship to visuals, specific copy creation, narrative role positioning
    90	
    91	## 6. Voice Catalog And Selection Rules
    92	
    93	When `audio_produce` is used in post-production, select `voice_id` from the approved catalog below. Do not invent ad-hoc voice choices when a catalog voice already fits.
    94	
    95	### Default Selection Rule
    96	
    97	Apply this order:
    98	- Match the **language** first.
    99	- Then match any explicit **accent** request (Beijing Mandarin, Taiwan Mandarin, British English, American English, Kanto Japanese).
   100	- Then match the requested **role / tone**: product intro, brand story, social short video, formal explainer, poetic narration, luxury brand, documentary, tutorial, etc.
   101	- If the brief does not specify voice gender/age/accent, use the language default marked below.

   171	
   172	### Hard Rules
   173	
   174	- Do not switch voices mid-video unless the creative brief explicitly calls for multiple speakers.
   175	- When a multi-sequence video uses post voiceover, keep the same `voice_id` across all VO segments unless there is an intentional speaker change.
   176	- If the user explicitly asks for a specific accent, prioritize accent match over generic style fit.
   177	- If the user provides a voice reference requirement that the current tool path cannot satisfy, surface that limitation instead of silently substituting an arbitrary voice.

If no catalog voice fits the brief, select the closest match by language → accent → style and note the approximation — never use an unlisted ID.

content.text:
Now let me design the production plan, then generate all 4 segments in parallel.

**Production Plan:**

- **Model**: Seedance (L3 — pure text-driven, no user references, motion graphics style narrative)
- **Strategy**: Parallel generation — 4 independent scenes, no visual continuity between them
- **Visual Style Convention**: Clean 2D flat vector motion graphics, corporate blue (#1E5BDB range) and green (#27AE60 range), soft studio-white background lighting, sharp clean digital texture
- **Audio**: VO is off-screen narration → TTS path. Videos generated `sound: on` for SFX atmosphere only, no BGM in generation. BGM post-produced (video-first mode).
- **Voice**: English corporate/commercial → **Bella** (`hpp4J3VqNfWAUOO0d1Us`) — professional, bright, product intro

**Sequence Breakdown:**

| Seq | Duration | Content | VO |
|-----|----------|---------|-----|
| S1 | 15s | Confused 2D character with tax docs → papers fly → consultant icon appears | "Is managing corporate taxes slowing down your business growth?" |
| S2 | 15s | Infographic: calendar → folder lock → calculator glow, 3-step animation | "Our streamlined service makes it easy. Step one: Book a call..." |
| S3 | 15s | Green checkmark over financial report, character now confident + thumbs up | "You get clear, compliant financial reports, giving you peace of mind." |
| S4 | 10s | Pulsing CTA button on blue background | "Focus on your business, let us handle the numbers. Book your free consultation today." |

seedance_reference2video.prompt: Clean 2D flat vector motion graphics style, corporate blue and white color palette, soft even studio lighting, crisp sharp digital texture. [0-5s] A cheerful cartoon 2D character stands center frame in 9:16 vertical layout, holding a comically massive teetering stack of colorful tax documents and folders. The character has a confused, overwhelmed expression — wide eyes, sweat droplets, question marks floating around. Warm blue corporate background. [5-10s] The giant pile of papers suddenly bursts and flies outward in all directions — animated pages scatter across the screen with a satisfying swooshing sound effect. The character stumbles backward with a surprised expression. Corporate blue background. [10-15s] The scattered papers fade away. A friendly glowing icon of a professional consultant — a clean circular avatar with a briefcase and a warm smile — smoothly animates in from the center with a sparkle effect. The character looks relieved and hopeful. Clean white and blue background. No background music, no musical score, no musical instruments — sound effects and ambient audio only.

Clean 2D flat vector motion graphics style, corporate blue and green color palette, soft even studio lighting, crisp sharp digital texture. A smooth infographic animation on a clean white background in 9:16 vertical format. [0-5s] A bold title card slides in: Step 1. A large glowing calendar icon animates in from above with a bouncy ease-in, landing in the center. The word Booking appears beneath it in clean corporate blue sans-serif text. Small animated clock hands and checkmarks pulse around the icon. [5-12s] The calendar smoothly slides up and a Step 2 card appears. A folder icon animates in, then a padlock snaps shut over it with a satisfying click visual effect. The label Document Handover appears in green text below. Security shield sparkle effects animate around it. [12-15s] Step 2 slides up and Step 3 arrives. A glowing calculator icon appears, with animated data lines and graph bars rising up around it. The label Processing glows in corporate green. All three step icons are now visible in a clean stacked vertical sequence. No background music, no musical score, no musical instruments — sound effects and ambient audio only.

You get clear, compliant financial reports, giving you peace of mind.
Focus on your business, let us handle the numbers. Book your free consultation today.

The core issues:

1. **Scene 1 subtitle overflows** — "Is managing corporate taxes slowing down your business growth?" is a single long line (~62 chars) at fontsize 30 on a 720px canvas. It exceeds the safe ~600px stage width. Need to split into two shorter cues.
2. **VO-to-subtitle sync** — I'll re-derive precise character-aligned split points from the subtitle JSON data I already have, and use two-line wraps wherever any cue risks overflow.

**Safe-area math for 720px wide, fontsize 30, Inter 500:**
- ~16px per character at size 30 → 62 chars × 16 ≈ 992px → way over 620px protected width
- Max ~36 chars per line safely → split long lines at phrase boundaries

Let me recalculate all subtitle cues with safe wrapping:

**Scene 1 VO1** (3.27s, starts at 1000ms):
- "Is managing corporate taxes" → 0-1.515s → abs 1000–2515ms

**Scene 2 VO2** (10.791s, starts at 15500ms) — already split into 4 phrases, but checking lengths:
- "Our streamlined service makes it easy." — 38 chars → ~608px borderline, reduce fontsize to 26 or split
- "Step one: Book a call." — 22 chars ✓
- "Step two: Securely upload your documents." — 42 chars → split needed
- "Step three: We handle the complex processing." — 46 chars → split needed

**Scene 3 VO3** — already split into 2 lines ✓ both short enough

- "Focus on your business, let us handle the numbers." — 51 chars → split

I'll use fontsize **26** and a protected width of **620px** (~40 chars max), and split any line over 38 chars. Let me use the character timing data to find precise split points.

content.name: write_todos
content.description:
Use this tool to create and manage a structured task list for your current work session. This helps you track progress, organize complex tasks, and demonstrate thoroughness to the user.

Only use this tool if you think it will be helpful in staying organized. If the user's request is trivial and takes less than 3 steps, it is better to NOT use this tool and just do the task directly.

## When to Use This Tool
Use this tool in these scenarios:

1. Complex multi-step tasks - When a task requires 3 or more distinct steps or actions
2. Non-trivial and complex tasks - Tasks that require careful planning or multiple operations
3. User explicitly requests todo list - When the user directly asks you to use the todo list
4. User provides multiple tasks - When users provide a list of things to be done (numbered or comma-separated)
5. The plan may need future revisions or updates based on results from the first few steps

## How to Use This Tool
1. When you start working on a task - Mark it as in_progress BEFORE beginning work.
2. After completing a task - Mark it as completed and add any new follow-up tasks discovered during implementation.
3. You can also update future tasks, such as deleting them if they are no longer necessary, or adding new tasks that are necessary. Don't change previously completed tasks.
4. You can make several updates to the todo list at once. For example, when you complete a task, you can mark the next task you need to start as in_progress.

## When NOT to Use This Tool
It is important to skip using this tool when:
1. There is only a single, straightforward task
2. The task is trivial and tracking it provides no benefit
3. The task can be completed in less than 3 trivial steps
4. The task is purely conversational or informational

## Task States and Management

1. **Task States**: Use these states to track progress:
   - pending: Task not yet started
   - in_progress: Currently working on (you can have multiple tasks in_progress at a time if they are not related to each other and can be run in parallel)
   - completed: Task finished successfully

2. **Task Management**:
   - Update task status in real-time as you work
   - Mark tasks complete IMMEDIATELY after finishing (don't batch completions)
   - Complete current tasks before starting new ones
   - Remove tasks that are no longer relevant from the list entirely
   - IMPORTANT: When you write this todo list, you should mark your first task (or tasks) as in_progress immediately!.
   - IMPORTANT: Unless all tasks are completed, you should always have at least one task in_progress to show the user that you are working on something.

3. **Task Completion Requirements**:
   - ONLY mark a task as completed when you have FULLY accomplished it
   - If you encounter errors, blockers, or cannot finish, keep the task as in_progress
   - When blocked, create a new task describing what needs to be resolved
   - Never mark a task as completed if:
     - There are unresolved issues or errors
     - Work is partial or incomplete
     - You encountered blockers that prevent completion
     - You couldn't find necessary resources or dependencies
     - Quality standards haven't been met

4. **Task Breakdown**:
   - Create specific, actionable items
   - Break complex tasks into smaller, manageable steps
   - Use clear, descriptive task names

Being proactive with task management demonstrates attentiveness and ensures you complete all requirements successfully
Remember: If you only need to make a few tool calls to complete a task, and it is clear what you need to do, it is better to just do the task directly and NOT call this tool at all.

items.description: A single todo item with content and status.

content.name: ls
content.description:
Lists all files in a directory.

This is useful for exploring the filesystem and finding the right file to read or edit.
You should almost ALWAYS use this tool before using the read_file or edit_file tools.

path.description: Absolute path to the directory to list. Must be absolute, not relative.

content.name: read_file
content.description:
Reads a file from the filesystem.

Assume this tool is able to read all files. If the User provides a path to a file assume that path is valid. It is okay to read a file that does not exist; an error will be returned.

Usage:
- By default, it reads up to 100 lines starting from the beginning of the file
- **IMPORTANT for large files and codebase exploration**: Use pagination with offset and limit parameters to avoid context overflow
  - First scan: read_file(path, limit=100) to see file structure
  - Read more sections: read_file(path, offset=100, limit=200) for next 200 lines
  - Only omit limit (read full file) when necessary for editing
- Specify offset and limit: read_file(path, offset=0, limit=100) reads first 100 lines
- Results are returned using cat -n format, with line numbers starting at 1
- Lines longer than 5,000 characters will be split into multiple lines with continuation markers (e.g., 5.1, 5.2, etc.). When you specify a limit, these continuation lines count towards the limit.
- You have the capability to call multiple tools in a single response. It is always better to speculatively read multiple files as a batch that are potentially useful.
- If you read a file that exists but has empty contents you will receive a system reminder warning in place of file contents.
- Image files (`.png`, `.jpg`, `.jpeg`, `.gif`, `.webp`, etc.), audio and video files, and PDFs are returned as multimodal content blocks (see https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/langchain/messages#multimodal).

For multimodal reads (image, audio, video, PDF, etc.):
- Use `read_file(file_path=...)`
- Do NOT use `offset`/`limit` for images (pagination is text-only)
- If file details were compacted from history, call `read_file` again on the same path

- You should ALWAYS make sure a file has been read before editing it.

file_path.description: Absolute path to the file to read. Must be absolute, not relative.

offset.description: Line number to start reading from (0-indexed). Use for pagination of large files.

limit.description: Maximum number of lines to read. Use for pagination of large files.

content.name: write_file
content.description:
Writes to a new file in the filesystem.

Usage:
- The write_file tool will create the a new file.
- Prefer to edit existing files (with the edit_file tool) over creating new ones when possible.

file_path.description: Absolute path where the file should be created. Must be absolute, not relative.

content.description: The text content to write to the file. This parameter is required.

content.name: edit_file
content.description:
Performs exact string replacements in files.

Usage:
- You must read the file before editing. This tool will error if you attempt an edit without reading the file first.
- When editing, preserve the exact indentation (tabs/spaces) from the read output. Never include line number prefixes in old_string or new_string.
- ALWAYS prefer editing existing files over creating new ones.
- Only use emojis if the user explicitly requests it.

file_path.description: Absolute path to the file to edit. Must be absolute, not relative.

old_string.description: The exact text to find and replace. Must be unique in the file unless replace_all is True.

new_string.description: The text to replace old_string with. Must be different from old_string.

replace_all.description: If True, replace all occurrences of old_string. If False (default), old_string must be unique.

content.name: glob
content.description:
Find files matching a glob pattern.

Supports standard glob patterns: `*` (any characters), `**` (any directories), `?` (single character).
Returns a list of absolute file paths that match the pattern.

Examples:
- `**/*.py` - Find all Python files
- `*.txt` - Find all text files in root
- `/subdir/**/*.md` - Find all markdown files under /subdir

path.description: Base directory to search from. Defaults to root '/'.

content.name: grep
content.description:
Search for a text pattern across files.

Searches for literal text (not regex) and returns matching files or content based on output_mode.
Special characters like parentheses, brackets, pipes, etc. are treated as literal characters, not regex operators.

Examples:
- Search all files: `grep(pattern="TODO")`
- Search Python files only: `grep(pattern="import", glob="*.py")`
- Show matching lines: `grep(pattern="error", output_mode="content")`
- Search for code with special chars: `grep(pattern="def __init__(self):")`

pattern.description: Text pattern to search for (literal string, not regex).

path.description: Directory to search in. Defaults to current working directory.

glob.description: Glob pattern to filter which files to search (e.g., '*.py').

output_mode.description: Output format: 'files_with_matches' (file paths only, default), 'content' (matching lines with context), 'count' (match counts per file).

content.name: add_attachments
content.description: Share files (images, videos, audio, documents) with the user. Use for individual shots, segments, re-generated clips, audio, images, or any reference material. Also use when showing multiple files that are not mutually exclusive alternatives. Do not use for assembled videos (use show_final_video) or creative-direction choices (use show_preview_videos). Pass a list of virtual file paths starting with /workspace/.

file_paths.description: List of virtual file paths (e.g. /workspace/xxx) to attach

content.name: show_final_video
content.description: Deliver an assembled video representing the current version of the project. Use when you have composed a video from segments (with or without audio) and are presenting it as a complete viewing experience. Do not use for individual shots or segments. Pass a single virtual file path starting with /workspace/.

file_path.description: Virtual file path (e.g. /workspace/xxx) of the final video to display

content.name: show_preview_videos
content.description: Present 2+ videos as alternatives for the user to choose between. Use only when the options are mutually exclusive and the user needs to select one direction. Do not use for showing segments that will all be used, or for assembled results. Pass a list of virtual file paths starting with /workspace/.

file_paths.description: List of virtual file paths (e.g. /workspace/xxx) for preview videos. Provide 2+ videos for user to compare and choose.

content.name: analyze_file_content
content.description: Use Gemini to analyze image, video, or audio file content (multimodal understanding). Only supports image/*, video/*, audio/* MIME types. Pass virtual path (e.g. /workspace/xxx)and a query describing what to analyze.

query.description: Question or prompt to ask Gemini for analyzing the file content

content.name: summarize_url
content.description: Read and summarize (or answer questions about) the content at a single given URL. Requires a 'query' (e.g. 'summarize', 'list main points'). Use when you have a specific webpage URL. Does NOT search the web; only processes the provided URL.

url.description: The URL of the webpage to read and summarize

query.description: Instruction for how to process the content (e.g. summarize, list main points). Required.

model_name.description: Gemini model to use for content understanding

content.name: get_file_info
content.description: Get file information by virtual path. Returns: file_name, file_size, file_format, signed_url. Pass a virtual path (e.g. /workspace/assets/xxx_image.png).

content.name: video-editor__execute_edit_video
content.description: Execute video editing. Accepts a DSL object describing output settings and tracks, renders the result, and returns the output file reference.

name.description: Optional job name used only for debugging and logs.

fps.description: Output frames per second. Use 1 for single-frame PNG export.

format.description: Output format. Use 'mp4' for video or 'png' for a single still image; PNG requires exactly one rendered frame.

kind.description: Track kind: video, audio, text, subtitle, or overlay.

source_type.description: Clip source type. Omit or use 'file' for file input; use 'color' to generate a solid-color clip.

color.description: Solid color value for source_type=color, for example '#000000'.

speed.description: Playback speed multiplier. 2.0 is 2x faster; 0.5 is half speed.

x.description: Canvas X offset in pixels for overlay placement.

y.description: Canvas Y offset in pixels for overlay placement.

width.description: Rendered clip width in pixels after layout scaling.

height.description: Rendered clip height in pixels after layout scaling.

scale.description: Uniform scale multiplier relative to the natural or layout size.

z.description: Z order for overlays; higher values render on top.

crop.description: Crop rectangle applied before scaling and placement.

volume.description: Linear volume multiplier for this clip.

dbfs.description: Target loudness adjustment in dBFS for this clip.

font.description: Font family id returned by list_fonts.

font_weight.description: Optional font weight. Prefer numeric values like '400', '500', '700'.

fontsize.description: Font size in pixels for drawtext fontsize=.

fontcolor.description: Text color for drawtext fontcolor= (named color or #RRGGBB).

x.description: Horizontal anchor for drawtext x=. Use FFmpeg expressions, e.g. (w-text_w)/2 to center, or a pixel offset string like 100.

y.description: Vertical anchor for drawtext y=. Use FFmpeg expressions, e.g. h-100 to place text near the bottom, or (h-text_h)/2 for vertical center.

borderw.description: Outline width in pixels for drawtext borderw=.

bordercolor.description: Outline color for drawtext bordercolor= (named or hex).

shadowx.description: Shadow X offset in pixels (drawtext shadowx=). Typically set together with shadowy.

shadowy.description: Shadow Y offset in pixels (drawtext shadowy=). Typically set together with shadowx.

shadowcolor.description: Shadow color for drawtext shadowcolor= (named or hex).

box.description: Enable text background box: set to '1' to enable.

boxcolor.description: Background box color for drawtext boxcolor= (e.g. '#000000@0.55').

boxborderw.description: Padding around text inside the background box for drawtext boxborderw=.

alignment.description: ASS numpad alignment for subtitle tracks (1-9). 2=bottom-center (default), 5=center, 8=top-center.

margin_v.description: Vertical margin in pixels for subtitle tracks (distance from edge). Default 24.

text_opts.description: Text styling and position options for text or subtitle rendering.

file.description: Input file reference string. Required for file-backed video/audio/subtitle/overlay clips.

fps.description: Override source FPS when input metadata is missing or wrong.

in_ms.description: Inclusive source-local start time in milliseconds; not a timeline position. Defaults to 0.

out_ms.description: Exclusive source-local end time in milliseconds; not a timeline position. Clip duration is out_ms - in_ms.

source.description: File-backed source reference and source-local trim. Omit for text or source_type=color clips.

start_ms.description: Timeline placement start time in milliseconds. Omit for sequential placement after the previous clip on the same track. Timeline end = start_ms + (out_ms - in_ms), after speed.

end_ms.description: Timeline placement end time in milliseconds. Use for text/subtitle/overlay and source_type=color clips. File-backed video/audio clips get duration from source.out_ms instead.

e.description: Interpolation easing from this keyframe to the next one.

ms.description: Time in milliseconds relative to the start of the clip.

scale_kf.description: Scale keyframes applied over the clip duration.

e.description: Interpolation easing from this keyframe to the next one.

ms.description: Time in milliseconds relative to the start of the clip.

speed_kf.description: Playback speed keyframes applied over the clip duration.

e.description: Interpolation easing from this keyframe to the next one.

ms.description: Time in milliseconds relative to the start of the clip.

opacity_kf.description: Opacity keyframes applied over the clip duration.

e.description: Interpolation easing from this keyframe to the next one.

ms.description: Time in milliseconds relative to the start of the clip.

e.description: Interpolation easing from this keyframe to the next one.

ms.description: Time in milliseconds relative to the start of the clip.

clips.description: Clips on this track. For video/audio tracks they play in order unless start_ms is set.

in_ms.description: Incoming transition duration in milliseconds when in/out lengths differ.

out_ms.description: Outgoing transition duration in milliseconds when in/out lengths differ.

transitions.description: Transitions between adjacent clips on a video track. Expected length is len(clips)-1.

tracks.description: Timeline tracks. Video/audio clips are sequential within a track; text, subtitle, and overlay clips can be placed with start_ms.

content.name: video-editor__ffprobe
content.description: Probe a media file and return format/stream information using ffprobe. The file parameter should be a file reference string.

extra_args.description: Optional extra ffprobe arguments as a JSON array of strings.

file.description: File reference for the media to inspect.

content.name: video-editor__list_fonts
content.description: List all available preset font families. Returns each family's id (use this as text_opts.font), display name, supported languages, available weights, and styles.

content.name: audio_produce
content.description:
Unified audio generation tool.

Hard rules:
1) Top-level fields provider/mode/model are required.
2) provider_param must contain exactly one capability block key.
3) Do not place provider/mode/model in provider_param.
4) Inline audio tags such as [excited] and [whispers] are supported in text.

mode.description: Generation mode. Must match the selected provider_param block.

model.description: Provider model identifier. Must match the selected provider_param block.

name.description: Asset name used when uploading. 1-30 chars, letters/digits/underscore/hyphen only (regex: ^[A-Za-z0-9_-]{1,30}$).

provider.description: Provider identifier. Must match the selected provider_param block.

provider_param.description: Required. Provider-specific parameters only (do not include name/provider/mode/model here). Pass exactly one block key. Allowed keys: elevenlabs_text2speech.

elevenlabs_text2speech.description: ElevenLabs text-to-speech (v3) with character-level timestamp alignment. Server-managed: model_id=eleven_v3, output_format=mp3_44100_128, apply_text_normalization=auto. Only for provider=elevenlabs, mode=text2speech, model in [eleven_v3].

language_code.description: ISO 639-1 language code (e.g. en, zh, ja, ko, fr, de). When omitted, the model auto-detects from text. Recommended for non-English or mixed-language content.

seed.description: Deterministic seed, 0-4294967295. Same seed with identical parameters produces approximately the same output.

similarity_boost.description: Adherence to the original voice timbre, 0.0-1.0. Higher values replicate the reference voice more closely.

speed.description: Speech rate multiplier, 0.5-2.0. 1.0 is normal speed.

stability.description: Voice consistency, 0.0-1.0. Lower values produce more expressive variation; higher values produce steadier, more monotone output.

text.description: Text to convert to speech. Supports inline audio tags for emotion control in lowercase brackets: [excited], [whispers], [sad], [laughs], [sighs], [shouts]. Tags can precede a sentence or appear mid-sentence, e.g. '[nervous] Is anyone there? [whispers] I thought I heard something...'

voice_id.description: ElevenLabs voice identifier from the preset voice catalog.

content.name: image_generate
content.description:
Unified image generation tool.

Model guidance:
- Use gemini when you need consistency-preserving reference workflows, realistic rendering, image-to-image iteration, or packaging/key-frame generation. Prefer it when a reliable visual reference already exists and the goal is to extend, adapt, or refine it while keeping the result stable.
- Use seedream when you need high-aesthetic hero visuals, fine-detail rendering, polished brand/product imagery, or a more visually finished result from either text or image input.

Hard rules:
1) Top-level fields provider/mode/model are required.
2) provider_param must contain exactly one capability block key.
3) Do not place provider/mode/model in provider_param.
4) Unknown fields inside provider_param are rejected.
5) Do not mix video/music-only fields into image blocks (for example duration, duration_seconds, aspect_ratio for non-supported models, or music_length_ms).

mode.description: Generation mode. Must match the selected provider_param block.

model.description: Provider model identifier. Must match the selected provider_param block.

name.description: Asset name used when uploading. 1-30 chars, letters/digits/underscore/hyphen only (regex: ^[A-Za-z0-9_-]{1,30}$).

provider.description: Provider identifier. Must match the selected provider_param block.

provider_param.description: Required. Provider-specific parameters only (do not include name/provider/mode/model here). Pass exactly one block key. Allowed keys: seedream_text2image, seedream_image2image, gemini_text2image, gemini_image2image.

gemini_image2image.description: Only for provider=gemini, mode=image2image, model in [gemini-3.1-flash-image-preview]. Generate image via Google Gemini / Nano Banana 2 (Gemini 3.1 Flash Image). Best for consistency-preserving reference workflows, realistic rendering, image-to-image iteration, and packaging/key-frame generation. Use when a reliable visual reference already exists and the goal is to extend, adapt, or refine it while keeping the result stable. Especially strong for editing or extending an existing image without drifting too far from the source.

file.description: Path to the source image as provided by the runtime, pointing at an image (not video/audio).

prompt.description: Gemini image editing prompt. Describe exactly how to transform the source image.

response_modalities.description: Response modalities. For image generation, include IMAGE.

gemini_text2image.description: Only for provider=gemini, mode=text2image, model in [gemini-3.1-flash-image-preview]. Generate image via Google Gemini / Nano Banana 2 (Gemini 3.1 Flash Image). Best for consistency-preserving reference workflows, realistic rendering, image-to-image iteration, and packaging/key-frame generation. Use when a reliable visual reference already exists and the goal is to extend, adapt, or refine it while keeping the result stable. Also suitable for text-to-image tasks that benefit from strong composition and text rendering.

response_modalities.description: Response modalities. For image generation, include IMAGE.

seedream_image2image.description: Only for provider=seedream, mode=image2image, model in [seedream-4-5-251128]. Generate image via Volcengine Seedream 4.5. Best for high-aesthetic hero visuals, fine-detail rendering, polished brand/product imagery, and high-quality text-to-image or image-to-image generation. Use when the goal is a more refined, delicate, and visually finished result, whether starting from text or upgrading an existing visual reference. Especially suitable when an existing image needs to be elevated into a more polished final visual.

file.description: Path to the source image as provided by the runtime.

guidance_scale.description: Guidance scale (optional). Must be between 1.0 and 10.0; recommended around 2.5 for subtle edits. Do not send values outside this range.

prompt.description: Describe the desired edits in detail.

response_format.description: Response format. url=direct image URL, b64_json=base64 image data.

size.description: Output size. <width>x<height> or preset (2k, 4k). seedream-4-5: 1k not supported, custom dimensions must satisfy w×h ≥ 3,686,400 (e.g. 1920x1920). seedream-4-0: 1k/2k/4k all supported. Default 2k.

seedream_text2image.description: Only for provider=seedream, mode=text2image, model in [seedream-4-5-251128]. Generate image via Volcengine Seedream 4.5. Best for high-aesthetic hero visuals, fine-detail rendering, polished brand/product imagery, and high-quality text-to-image or image-to-image generation. Use when the goal is a more refined, delicate, and visually finished result, whether starting from text or upgrading an existing visual reference.

prompt.description: Describe the target image in detail, max 2500 chars.

response_format.description: Response format. url=direct image URL, b64_json=base64 image data.

size.description: Output size. <width>x<height> or preset (2k, 4k). seedream-4-5: 1k not supported, custom dimensions must satisfy w×h ≥ 3,686,400 (e.g. 1920x1920). seedream-4-0: 1k/2k/4k all supported. Default 2k.

content.name: music_generate
content.description:
Unified music generation tool.

Hard rules:
1) Top-level fields provider/mode/model are required.
2) provider_param must contain exactly one capability block key.
3) Do not place provider/mode/model in provider_param.
4) Unknown fields inside provider_param are rejected.
5) Do not mix image/video-only fields into music blocks (for example size, resolution, aspect_ratio, duration, or duration_seconds).

mode.description: Generation mode. Must match the selected provider_param block.

model.description: Provider model identifier. Must match the selected provider_param block.

name.description: Asset name used when uploading. 1-30 chars, letters/digits/underscore/hyphen only (regex: ^[A-Za-z0-9_-]{1,30}$).

provider.description: Provider identifier. Must match the selected provider_param block.

provider_param.description: Required. Provider-specific parameters only (do not include name/provider/mode/model here). Pass exactly one block key. Allowed keys: elevenlabs_text2music.

elevenlabs_text2music.description: Only for provider=elevenlabs, mode=text2music, model in [music_v1].

composition_plan.description: Structured composition plan with styles and sections. Exactly one of `prompt` or `composition_plan` must be set.

negative_global_styles.description: Free-form style tags to avoid across the whole song.

positive_global_styles.description: Free-form style tags to include across the whole song.

sections.description: Ordered song sections with per-section styles, duration, and optional lyrics.

lines.description: Lyrics lines for this section. For non-instrumental generation, this field is required and should contain at least one line (use [""] as a placeholder if needed). For instrumental generation, use [].

negative_local_styles.description: Free-form style tags to avoid in this section, for example ['slow tempo', 'acoustic guitar'].

positive_local_styles.description: Free-form style tags to include in this section, for example ['upbeat pop', 'electronic drums'].

section_name.description: Section label such as intro, verse, chorus, bridge, or outro.

duration_ms.description: Requested length in milliseconds. Valid range is 10000-300000 (for example 30s = 30000). Use `duration_ms` in this schema block.

force_instrumental.description: Generate instrumental output only. CANNOT be used with `composition_plan`, only be used with `prompt`.

prompt.description: High-level music prompt text. Exactly one of `prompt` or `composition_plan` must be set.

content.name: video_generate
content.description:
Unified video generation tool.

Hard rules:
1) Top-level fields provider/mode/model are required.
2) provider_param must contain exactly one capability block key.
3) Do not place provider/mode/model in provider_param.
4) Unknown fields inside provider_param are rejected.
5) Do not mix image/music-only fields into video blocks (for example size, seed, music_length_ms, or output_format).

mode.description: Generation mode. Must match the selected provider_param block.

model.description: Provider model identifier. Must match the selected provider_param block.

name.description: Asset name used when uploading. 1-30 chars, letters/digits/underscore/hyphen only (regex: ^[A-Za-z0-9_-]{1,30}$).

provider.description: Provider identifier. Must match the selected provider_param block.

provider_param.description: Required. Provider-specific parameters only (do not include name/provider/mode/model here). Pass exactly one block key. Allowed keys: kling_reference2video, fal_grok_text2video, seedance_reference2video.

fal_grok_text2video.description: Only for provider=fal, mode=text2video, model in [xai/grok-imagine-video/text-to-video].

aspect_ratio.description: Aspect ratio, for example 16:9, 9:16, or 1:1. Pass a provider-supported ratio when provided.

duration_seconds.description: Duration in seconds. Must be between 1 and 15 when provided.

prompt.description: Prompt for Grok text-to-video. Do not send image/audio-only fields in this block.

resolution.description: Resolution. Must be exactly 480p or 720p when provided; 1080p is not supported.

kling_reference2video.description: Only for provider=kling, mode=reference2video, model in [kling-v3-omni].

aspect_ratio.description: Output aspect ratio. Required for create video. For edit video (refer_type=base), it can be omitted and output follows the input video's ratio. If video_list is provided with refer_type=feature, aspect_ratio is still required.

duration.description: Output duration in seconds. Without video_list: 3-15. With video_list and refer_type=feature: 3-10 only. With refer_type=base: ignored, because output duration follows the input video duration.

image_list.description: Reference images for guiding visual direction. Every image must have a clear role declaration in the prompt (for example product reference, character reference, style reference). Capacity rules: up to 7 images when video_list is empty; up to 4 images when video_list is also provided.

file.description: Path to a reference image as provided by the runtime. Prefer original JPG/PNG (not thumbnails), at least 300x300.

prompt.description: Free-form storyboard prompt. For create video (refer_type=feature or no video_list): explicitly map every reference asset to its role, for example 'image 1 provides the character', 'image 2 provides the style', 'video 1 provides the camera motion'. Then describe subject actions, camera movement, timing cues, atmosphere, and SFX direction. For edit video (refer_type=base): describe what to change and what style to apply while preserving the input video's structure. Do not put media references inside the prompt; use image_list/video_list item file fields.

sound.description: Audio co-generation switch. Must be 'off' whenever video_list is provided. Use 'on' only for create-video requests without video input. When 'on', synchronized SFX can be generated; for a single continuous shot <=15s, music/dialogue may also be co-generated. For multi-shot sequences, only SFX is reliable; music and dialogue should usually be handled in post.

video_list.description: Reference or edit video input. At most 1 item per call. When provided, sound must be off, audio co-generation is unavailable, and image_list capacity drops to 4.

file.description: Path to reference or edit video (mp4/mov) as provided by the runtime. At most 1 item; input should be <=10s.

keep_original_sound.description: Preserve the input video's original audio. Only meaningful for edit video (refer_type=base); usually omit it for feature mode.

refer_type.description: Usage scenario. 'feature': create a new video while borrowing motion/camera/composition from the input video; duration must be 3-10 and sound must be off. 'base': edit the input video's visuals while preserving its structure/timing/motion; duration follows the input video, aspect_ratio can be omitted, and sound must be off.

seedance_reference2video.description: Only for provider=seedance, mode=reference2video, model in [doubao-seedance-2-0-260128, doubao-seedance-2-0-fast-260128].

audio_list.description: Reference audio clips. Up to 3 items; each clip 2–15 s, combined duration of all clips ≤ 15 s. Cannot be the sole input — at least one image or video must also be provided. Distinct from sound (on/off), which controls generated audio in the output video. Total reference items across image_list + video_list + audio_list must not exceed 12.

file.description: Path to a reference audio clip as provided by the runtime.

duration.description: Output video duration in whole seconds. Required. Must be 4–15 (string digits). Do not use -1 or omit.

image_list.description: Reference images. Up to 9 items. Total reference items across image_list + video_list + audio_list must not exceed 12.

file.description: Path to a reference image as provided by the runtime.

prompt.description: Text prompt for video generation. Supports Chinese and English; max ~500 Chinese characters or ~1000 English words. Can reference uploaded assets by position (e.g. 'image 1', 'video 2'). Double-quoted text is interpreted as dialogue for speech synthesis when sound is on. At least one of prompt, image_list, video_list, or audio_list is required; audio_list alone is not valid. Never place media references in prompt — use image_list/video_list/audio_list item file fields instead.

sound.description: Controls whether the output video has generated audio. 'on' (default): model generates voice, SFX, and BGM based on prompt and visuals; output is mono. 'off': silent video. Distinct from audio_list, which provides reference audio clips as input.

video_list.description: Reference video clips. Up to 3 items; each clip 2–15 s, combined duration of all clips ≤ 15 s. Total reference items across image_list + video_list + audio_list must not exceed 12.

file.description: Path to a reference video as provided by the runtime.

content.text: A 60-second vertical corporate explainer ad featuring clean 2D motion graphics that walks viewers from tax overwhelm to confident resolution across four scenes, with professional voiceover, synced subtitles, and an uplifting BGM — ending on a pulsing "Book Your Free Consultation" call-to-action.

messages.content:
Format: 9:16 aspect ratio, 60 seconds duration.
Visual Scene 1 (0-15s): An animated 2D character looks confused while holding a massive stack of tax documents. The papers suddenly fly away, replaced by a friendly "Consultant" icon.
Visual Scene 2 (15-35s): A smooth infographic animation shows three steps: 1) A calendar icon (Booking). 2) A folder icon securely locking (Document Handover). 3) A glowing calculator analyzing data (Processing).
Visual Scene 3 (35-50s): A green checkmark appears over a neatly organized digital financial report. The confused character is now confident and giving a thumbs-up.
Visual Scene 4 (50-60s): A bold "Book Your Free Consultation" button pulses on a clean blue background.
Style: Clean 2D vector motion graphics, corporate blue and green colors, smooth transitions.
Voiceover & Subtitles:
[0-15s] Voiceover: "Is managing corporate taxes slowing down your business growth?" / Subtitles: "Is managing corporate taxes slowing down your business growth?"
[15-35s] Voiceover: "Our streamlined service makes it easy. Step one: Book a call. Step two: Securely upload your documents. Step three: We handle the complex processing." / Subtitles: "Our streamlined service makes it easy. Step one: Book a call. Step two: Securely upload your documents. Step three: We handle the complex processing."
[35-50s] Voiceover: "You get clear, compliant financial reports, giving you peace of mind." / Subtitles: "You get clear, compliant financial reports, giving you peace of mind."
[50-60s] Voiceover: "Focus on your business, let us handle the numbers. Book your free consultation today." / Subtitles: "Focus on your business, let us handle the numbers. Book your free consultation today."

content.partial_json: {"file_path": "/.skills/0/generation-skill/SKILL.md"}

messages.content:
1	---
     2	name: script-skill
     3	description: "Sequence-level production design — break a creative script into executable shot sequences with complete visual, audio, editing, and effects design. Handles shot composition, camera language, SFX/dialogue/VO/music planning, visual packaging, production model routing (four-layer system), and production strategy. Use when a creative direction is locked and needs to be translated into a production-ready shot list. Not for initial creative exploration (brainstorm-skill), subject asset organization (subject-asset-skill), or video generation execution (generation-skill)."
     4	---
     5	
     6	# Usage
     7	
     8	**Input**: A creative script from brainstorm-skill — narrative arc, key beats, emotional shape, audio direction, visual style notes, plus user-uploaded reference assets and format preferences.
     9	
    10	**Output**: A production-ready **sequence breakdown** with per-shot design covering visuals, audio, editing rhythm, packaging, asset mapping, and model routing — carried forward as internal context for subject-asset-skill and generation-skill. This is the definitive execution plan: generation-skill follows it, not re-invents it.
    11	
    12	## Required Planning Output
    13	
    14	Before downstream production begins, produce a lightweight structured plan containing:
    15	
    16	- `constraint_summary`
    17	- `shot_strategy`
    18	- `audio_strategy`
    19	- `consistency_strategy`
    20	- `fallback_strategy`
    21	
    22	Definitions:
    23	
    24	- `constraint_summary`: separate **hard constraints**, **soft preferences**, and **negotiable details**.
    25	- `shot_strategy`: define how the final viewing experience is organized visually, including whether the piece is executed as a single generation, segmented continuation, or another valid production path.
    26	- `audio_strategy`: define how dialogue, narration, ambience, and music are handled, including whether audio is co-generated, post-produced, or mixed.
    27	- `consistency_strategy`: define how identity, style, lighting, color, costume/material state, and cross-sequence continuity are maintained.
    28	- `fallback_strategy`: define what degraded path is acceptable if model or tool limits prevent the ideal execution path.
    29	
    30	If target duration exceeds the single-generation limit of the selected model, segmented generation is allowed. In that case, explicitly define how continuity will be preserved across segments rather than assuming downstream skills will infer it.
    31	
    32	**Skip condition**: When the user provides a complete shot-by-shot brief with timing, reference mapping, and audio direction already specified, route to generation-skill directly.
    33	
    34	**CRITICAL — This skill is not optional**: When brainstorm-skill confirms a direction (from concept previews or text summary), **this skill must be completed before any generation begins**. Do not hand off to generation-skill with only the concept preview and the user's choice — generation-skill requires the full production plan. Skipping this step causes generation-skill to improvise the narrative structure, miss reference gaps, and produce inconsistent multi-segment videos.
    35	
    36	---
    37	
    38	# Core Workflow
    39	
    40	## Step 1 — Production Model Routing (four-layer system)
    41	
    42	Determine the production model based on user assets and creative requirements. This decision constrains all downstream planning (sequence duration, audio co-generation, prompt limits). The routing must land on a concrete production choice.
    43	
    44	### Routing Decision Tree
    45	
    46	Analyze user assets via multimodal analysis for copyrighted IP and reference precision needs:
    47	
    48	| Layer | Model | Provider/Param | When |
    49	|-------|-------|----------------|------|
    50	| **L1** | Grok | `fal` / `fal_grok_text2video` | Non-final pure T2V without precision needs, NSFW, or speed-priority. **Never valid when user-uploaded reference materials exist.** |
    51	| **L2** | Seedance Fast | `seedance` / `seedance_reference2video` | Quality previews, default pure T2V |
    52	| **L3** | Seedance | `seedance` / `seedance_reference2video` | **Production default** — text-driven, multimodal mixed, long narrative |
    53	| **L4** | Kling | `kling` / `kling_reference2video` | Copyrighted IP, readable in-frame text/UI, or selective micro-detail |
    54	
    55	### Routing Rules
    56	
    57	- **Default is Seedance** for nearly all reference-based and text-only cases (including real human face photos via subject reference).
    58	- **Grok exclusion**: Grok only supports `text2video` with no `image_list`/`video_list`/`audio_list`. Selecting Grok discards all user references. Only available for pure-text input.
    59	- **Face handling**: Seedance accepts faces (real human photos, illustration, anime, cartoon, AI-generated stylized characters) directly via subject reference. Pass the user's image into `image_list` unchanged. Do not pre-process faces into line art, do not mask or remove the face area, and do not auto-route to Kling on the basis of "real face" alone.
    60	- **Seedance continuity**: If a Seedance Fast preview works well, production will often stay on Seedance. Tighter framing alone is not a strong reason to move to Kling.
    61	- **Closeup guidance**: Hand closeups, face closeups, beauty shots, and tight crops fit Seedance across all visual styles. Kling is more suitable when readable text/UI or micro-detail accuracy becomes central.
    62	- **15s planning bias**: For character videos with reference images and total target duration ≤15s, a single Seedance production call is often the best starting point.
    63	- **Kling rationale**: If the handoff selects Kling, note a concrete reason such as `copyrighted IP`, `readable text/UI`, or `micro-detail edge case`. Avoid vague reasons like `closeup`, `real face`, `higher precision`, or `better quality`.
    64	- **User intent overrides**: User explicitly wants speed → Grok. User explicitly wants quality → Seedance.
    65	- **Model affects sequence planning**: Seedance allows ≤15s per sequence; Kling allows ≤10s; Grok allows ≤10s. The model choice must be locked before sequence breakdown begins.
    66	
    67	### Pre-call Model Verification (binding for generation-skill)
    68	
    69	The routing decision must specify all four fields consistently:
    70	
    71	| Routing decision | `provider` | `model` | `mode` | `provider_param` key |
    72	|-----------------|-----------|---------|--------|---------------------|
    73	| Seedance | `seedance` | `doubao-seedance-2-0-260128` | `reference2video` | `seedance_reference2video` |
    74	| Seedance Fast | `seedance` | `doubao-seedance-2-0-fast-260128` | `reference2video` | `seedance_reference2video` |
    75	| Kling | `kling` | `kling-v3-omni` | `reference2video` | `kling_reference2video` |
    76	| Grok | `fal` | `xai/grok-imagine-video/text-to-video` | `text2video` | `fal_grok_text2video` |
    77	
    78	## Step 2 — Sequence Planning
    79	
    80	Break the creative script into executable sequences. Each sequence is a self-contained narrative unit that maps to one generation call.
    81	
    82	1. **Set sequence boundaries** — duration per sequence depends on the model: Seedance ≤15s, Kling ≤10s, Grok ≤10s. Let the narrative arc's natural break points drive where sequences split, not a fixed formula.
    83	
    84	2. **Single-call preference**: When total video duration fits within one call (Seedance ≤15s, Kling ≤10s), prefer a single sequence — use prompt-internal timing cues (`[0–5s]`, `[5–10s]`, etc.) to structure beats within a single prompt. Splitting is allowed when there is a clear creative reason (independent scenes, different reference sets, isolated retry needs), but unnecessary splitting wastes API calls, introduces cross-segment consistency risk, and forces post-assembly overhead. For multi-sequence videos, leverage Seedance's 15s cap to reduce sequence count.
    85	
    86	3. **Plan production strategy**:
    87	   - **Parallel** (default) — all sequences generated independently from shared references. Faster, every sequence gets audio co-generation.
    88	   - **Serial** — only when sequences depict continuous unbroken action in the same scene requiring frame-level visual continuity.
    89	   - **Hybrid** — serial for continuous-action blocks, parallel for the rest.
    90	   - **Continuous-action override**: When adjacent sequences depict a **single character's unbroken motion arc** — no scene change, no time skip — those sequences **must be recommended as serial**, regardless of reference coverage. Independent scenes can be parallel.
    91	   - Audio is **not** a factor — Seedance supports audio co-generation in serial continuation.
    92	   - Decide before any generation. State the choice and reasoning.
    93	
    94	## Step 3 — Per-Sequence Visual Design
    95	
    96	For each sequence, design the visual execution. Consult `references/shot-design-principles.md` for shot sequence logic, camera movement narrative functions, and shot connection patterns.
    97	
    98	1. **Shot outline** — core shots within the sequence: what happens, shot type (wide/medium/close/detail), camera movement (static/push-in/pan/orbit/tracking), and the narrative purpose of each shot.
    99	
   100	2. **Timing allocation** — budget time per shot using prompt-internal timing cues (`[0–5s]`, `[5–10s]`). Let the emotional weight drive duration: high-intensity beats get more time, breathing beats get less. For shots with voiceover or readable text, budget ≥ spoken/read duration + 0.5–1s slack (~3–4 Chinese chars/sec, ~2.5–3 English words/sec). See `references/editing-rhythm-design.md` for rhythm curve design and how emotion/information density maps to shot language.
   101	
   102	3. **Emotion & information density arc**: Annotate where each sequence sits on the emotional intensity and information density curves — whether a given moment is a quiet breathing beat, an information-heavy walkthrough, an emotional peak, etc. These two dimensions inform shot language choices (see `references/editing-rhythm-design.md` Section 4).
   103	
   104	4. **Visual Style Convention** (multi-sequence only) — define 4 constants that every sequence shares:
   105	   - **Rendering style** (photorealistic / cinematic / painterly / illustration / motion graphics)
   106	   - **Color palette** (warm/cool/neutral, dominant tones)
   107	   - **Lighting mood** (natural / studio / dramatic / soft / golden hour)
   108	   - **Texture quality** (organic film grain / clean digital / dreamy soft focus)
   109	
   110	   These constants become a **style preamble** that generation-skill prepends to every segment's prompt, preventing each segment from independently interpreting the visual style. When the creative story involves a deliberate style shift (e.g., reality → fantasy), annotate which segments deviate and why. If all segments share one visual world (the common case), state the convention once and note "applies to all sequences."
   111	
   112	5. **Subject consistency plan** — for every subject element (character, product, environment, brand element) appearing in more than one sequence, specify which reference image anchors it and confirm the reference will be passed to every relevant sequence. Detailed reference mapping and gap analysis happens in subject-asset-skill.
   113	
   114	## Step 4 — Audio Design
   115	
   116	Design the complete audio landscape. Audio co-generation is the #1 priority — SFX and on-screen dialogue are always co-generated, never post-produced. See `../generation-skill/references/audio-strategy.md` for the full audio strategy by model and reference combination.
   117	
   118	Before assigning any voice path, read the request through three lenses: **visible presenter speech**, **voiceover**, **mixed**. If the boundary is ambiguous, ask one focused question before planning.
   119	
   120	For each sequence:
   121	
   122	1. **SFX & atmosphere** — always co-generated. Design at least one concrete sound event tied to on-screen action per sequence. Plan a shared ambient foundation across sequences for cross-segment coherence. See `references/audio-design-guide.md` Sections 1–3 for sound-style positioning and audio-visual relationships.
   123	
   124	2. **SFX coherence plan** (multi-sequence only): Define a **shared ambient foundation** — a consistent base-layer SFX character that should persist across all segments (e.g., "warm organic room tone" or "soft outdoor nature ambience"). On top of this base, plan a **designed SFX arc** across segments — how the sound evolves through the narrative, not random per-segment choices. This plan feeds into generation-skill's per-segment SFX prompt writing.
   125	
   126	3. **Dialogue & monologue** (on-screen speech) — always co-generated when the character's speech starts and ends within the sequence. Design the spoken content, emotional register, and rhythm. See `references/dialogue-monologue-design-kb.md` Sections 1–2 for dialogue construction principles.
   127	
   128	4. **Voiceover & narration** (off-screen speech) — post-assembly via TTS for cross-sequence voice consistency. Plan the VO content, pacing, and tone per sequence. Select voice ID from the catalog. See `references/vo-design-principles.md` Sections 1–2 for VO content design and Section 5 for the voice catalog.
   129	
   130	5. **Reference-audio presenter path** — if supplied speech audio or a voice reference is meant to drive visible performance, start with audio-conditioned generation. Seedance often fits best: pass speech audio in `audio_list` with the presenter reference, and split long audio by sequence so each sequence gets only its own ≤15s chunk. If the voice should stay off-screen, keep it in the narration path above.
   131	
   132	6. **BGM timing mode** — determine temporal relationship between music and visuals:
   133	   - **Video-first** (default >15s): generate video first, analyze rhythm, compose BGM to match.
   134	   - **Music-first**: when the concept is rhythm-driven or user provided a music track.
   135	   - **Independent**: when BGM is purely ambient.
   136	   
   137	   Base the recommendation on the creative story's audio-visual relationship. See `../assembly-skill/references/assembly-rules.md` for BGM timing strategy and mix patterns.
   138	
   139	7. **Voice source exclusivity** — each spoken line has exactly one audio source (co-gen OR TTS, never both). Declare the assignment per line before any generation call.
   140	
   141	8. **Audio strategy by model/reference combination** (key decisions):
   142	   - **Seedance with any references** → audio co-gen enabled (`sound: "on"`), design SFX + dialogue in prompt.
   143	   - **Visible presenter + supplied speech/timbre reference** → usually prefer Seedance with per-sequence speech chunks in `audio_list`; this often preserves audio-visual coupling better than later narration.
   144	   - **Kling with reference video** → always silent, extract Sound Reference Context for post BGM/narration.
   145	   - **Single ≤15s Seedance / ≤10s Kling** → design dialogue/monologue + SFX in prompt; allow music + narration in-prompt only when this is the final single delivery.
   146	   - **Multi-sequence** → design SFX + in-sequence dialogue per sequence; keep BGM and off-screen narration for post-assembly.
   147	
   148	## Step 5 — Visual Packaging & Text Design
   149	
   150	Design all on-screen text, brand elements, UI, and decorative graphics. See `references/packaging-keyframe-guide.md` for the complete Layer 1/Layer 2 classification and composition rules, and `references/visual-packaging-guide.md` for overlay types and design patterns.
   151	
   152	### Two-Layer System
   153	
   154	**Layer 1 — AI Generation Layer (via key frame reference images)**: For packaging elements and environment-layer text carriers where visual atmosphere and scene integration matter more than pixel-perfect text accuracy.
   155	
   156	- **What belongs here**: Brand name/logo compositions, decorative typography / artistic text, visual style overlays, product packaging layouts, end cards, sticker-style elements, animated graphic overlays, plus **any in-scene material carrying readable text** (posters, signs, certificates, LED walls, dashboards, app screens, device interfaces, product labels, title cards, picture-in-picture-style screens).
   157	- **How it works**: Generate text-bearing key frame images using image generation tools. Pass to generation via `image_list`. In the prompt, describe how the image's content appears in the video — not the literal text.
   158	- **Readable text rule**: If the audience is supposed to read exact words on screen, those exact glyphs must live in a reference image. Do **not** plan to have the model draw the words from prompt text.
   159	- **Prompt prohibition**: Do not write instructions like `"the screen shows '90%'"` or `"the words XXX appear"` as if the video model should render characters from scratch.
   160	- **In-frame packaging**: Text that is visually integrated into the shot (poster-style headlines, spatially attached text, tracked typography) counts as Layer 1 key frame references.
   161	- **Product UI in scene**: Product screenshots, dashboards, app screens → environment-layer references, not post overlays.
   162	- **Text occlusion planning**: When text/UI may be partially covered, annotate the safer strategy (keep unobstructed, or provide dual-image reference).
   163	
   164	**Layer 2 — Post-Production Layer**: For precise readable timeline text composited after video generation.
   165	
   166	- **Supported when simple and safe-area bounded**: subtitles/captions, short titles, simple CTA lines, and low-density callouts can be planned as post-production text, provided the handoff states their role, approximate timing, platform safe-area tier, and whether they should be plain subtitle treatment or designed text.
   167	- **Still not generally supported as freeform dense overlay planning**: data-heavy information bars, prices, phone numbers, website links, complex multi-line claims, animated PiP/UI panels, or dense dashboard overlays should remain unsupported unless a concrete downstream edit path is explicitly confirmed.
   168	- **Creative script annotation**: use notes like `post_text: short CTA — supported if safe-area checked` or `packaging: data bar — NOT SUPPORTED without confirmed post overlay path`.
   169	
   170	### Classification Decision Tree
   171	
   172	- Brand name / logo → **Layer 1**
   173	- Artistic typography → **Layer 1**
   174	- Decorative stickers / graphic overlays → **Layer 1**
   175	- End card with brand identity → **Layer 1**
   176	- Product UI / dashboard / app screen in scene → **Layer 1** as `ui_ref`
   177	- Poster / sign / certificate / screen / label with readable text in scene → **Layer 1** as environment-layer key frame
   178	- Spatially integrated selling-point text in shot → **Layer 1** as in-frame packaging key frame
   179	- Short selling point text (must be readable) → **Layer 2** only when low-density and safe-area checked; otherwise **Layer 1** key frame or unsupported
   180	- Subtitles / captions → **Layer 2** with explicit **platform safe-area** planning
   181	- Data callouts / numbers → **Not supported** by default (offer Layer 1 as best-effort where appropriate)
   182	- Price / phone number / website link → **Not supported**
   183	
   184	## Step 6 — Pacing Plan
   185	
   186	Design how the overall rhythm flows across sequences (e.g., slow build → climax → resolution). Think about the emotional arc and information density across the full story before diving into individual shots — let the story's rhythm guide shot language choices rather than defaulting to aesthetic habit.
   187	
   188	## Step 7 — Production Handoff
   189	
   190	Package everything for subject-asset-skill and generation-skill as internal context (not shown to user):
   191	
   192	- **Sequence list** — ordered sequences with per-sequence: shot outline, timing, visual design, audio design, packaging elements, reference image assignments.
   193	- **Production model** — which model, with concrete `provider` + `model` + `mode` + `provider_param` key.
   194	- **Production strategy** — parallel / serial / hybrid, with reasoning.
   195	- **Visual Style Convention** — the 4 constants for multi-sequence consistency.
   196	- **Audio plan** — per-sequence SFX/dialogue assignment, VO plan, BGM timing mode, SFX coherence plan, voice source declarations.
   197	- **Packaging plan** — Layer 1 key frame image annotations, plus Layer 2 post-text items with role, approximate timing, platform safe-area tier, and density/feasibility notes.
   198	- **Packaging feasibility discipline** — when writing shot design and packaging notes, avoid concepts that rely on unsupported special characters, decorative Unicode, emoji typography, or mixed-font composition within one text line/track. Script-level packaging should already be production-aware, so downstream skills receive buildable design intent rather than impossible typography wishes.
   199	- **Text motion intent** — for subtitles, titles, and CTA text: define the role (informational caption vs visual packaging), motion intensity (none / restrained / expressive), and why it matches the video's tone. Do not specify implementation details such as ASS tags, keyframe arrays, coordinates, or exact animation durations.
   200	- **Pacing plan** — rhythm flow across sequences.
